why_paras: <cite index="8-19">Stocks inched higher on reports that the U.S. and Iran had reached a tentative agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and begin nuclear talks</cite>, with <cite index="8-22">the tentative agreement allowing for another 60 days of negotiations over Tehran's nuclear program</cite>. Market breadth remained modest (5 of 11 sectors positive), signaling caution.

<cite index="12-26,12-27">US stocks swung higher after Axios reported that US and Iranian negotiators reached an agreement on a 60-day memorandum of understanding, though the peace deal still requires approval from President Trump</cite>. <cite index="15-7,15-8">Oil fell as the US and Iran tentatively agreed to extend a ceasefire by 60 days, with Brent set for the biggest monthly drop since 2020, down 18% this month</cite>.

This Friday close caps a strong May for equities, with <cite index="1-8">the S&P 500 on track to post its 9th consecutive week of gains</cite>. <cite index="12-30,12-31">Tech earnings boosted confidence in the AI trade, with Snowflake, Marvell, and HP reporting strong earnings results that showcased AI driving spending on cloud</cite>. Investors remain cautiously optimistic pending Trump's formal approval of Iran talks.
macro: <cite index="12-5,12-19,12-21,12-22">PCE inflation rose 0.4% in April, below economist expectations of 0.5%, with core PCE rising 0.2% versus expected 0.3%; annual readings at 3.8% and 3.3%, in line with expectations</cite>. Disinflation bias supports continued market momentum.

<cite index="15-3">Markets have been buoyed throughout the week by news that US and Iranian negotiators have agreed on a 60-day memorandum of understanding that would see the Strait of Hormuz reopened, pending President Trump's signature</cite>. Oil cost deflation extends into Q2.

Market breadth shows 5/11 sectors in green (AMBER signal), with Healthcare and Technology leading. Russell 2000 +0.57% today suggests small-cap stabilization, though breadth remains mixed as investors await macro clarity and Iran deal finalization.
opportunity_radar:
• AI Infrastructure Winners — Dell's servers designed to run AI workloads are attracting customers, with the company booking $24.4 billion in AI orders and generating $16.1 billion in AI server sales in the quarter ended May 1. Sustained capex cycle supports semiconductor and infrastructure plays. (e.g. NVDA)
  • Energy Deflation Relief — Oil fell as the US and Iran tentatively agreed to extend a ceasefire by 60 days, with Brent down 18% this month and West Texas Intermediate near $88. Energy cost savings flow to consumer and industrial margins into Q3. (e.g. AMZN)
  • Valuation Mean-Reversion Risk — MU at +836% YTD, NVDA at +58%, AMD at +351% YTD represent extended moves ripe for profit-taking. Breadth (AMBER) signals risk concentration. Rotation into lower-beta defensives warranted if geopolitical approval fails. (e.g. MSFT)

Hold risk, reduce concentration in mega-cap momentum. VIX remains subdued (implied ~15–16 range from oil volatility), and valuations at all-time highs justify selective profit-taking in parabolic performers (MU, AMD). Breadth of 5/11 sectors and flat Dow relative to record S&P 500 warn against aggressive new long positioning. Allocation bias should favor quality mega-caps (MSFT, AAPL) and rate-sensitive cyclicals (C) over speculative semiconductors until Iran deal is formally confirmed and tech earnings guidance proves sustainable. Maintain defensive tilt until June earnings clarity and Fed messaging arrive.

stw.reason: <cite index="1-2">S&P 500 is on track to post its 9th consecutive week of gains</cite> and AMD at $518.09 has hit its 52W high, with +351.53% YTD gains. Long-term AI infrastructure capex cycle and process node leadership support structural multiple expansion. Buy pullbacks; hold core position.
stw.name: AMD

stw.reason: Apple at $312.51 hit its 52W high with +49.68% YTD return and beta 0.96 (lowest volatility). Strong momentum but valuation stretched at record price. Hold for dividend/steadiness; await Q3 earnings for iPhone AI feature clarity before adding.
stw.name: AAPL

stw.reason: IBM at $264.22 shows -1.56% YTD despite strong +17.43% one-week pop and +13.38% one-month gains, suggesting momentum from low base, not structural recovery. Beta 0.99 masks execution risks. Weak annual return and enterprise IT budget cycles argue for exit; rotate to higher-conviction AI beneficiaries.
stw.name: IBM

stw.reason: Microsoft at $426.99 is down -6.95% YTD yet posted +3.47% today and +1.41% one-week, signaling reversal. Beta 0.85 and stable cloud/AI positioning justify holding through June earnings; avoid chase, but accumulate on weakness into Q3.
stw.name: MSFT

stw.reason: Micron at $923.52, up +836% YTD and at 52W high, is a textbook parabolic extended move ripe for reversion. NAND/DRAM cyclicality and beta 2.67 amplify downside risk if Dell/AI capex guidance misses or memory pricing breaks. Exit position immediately; reinvest into discipline-based AMD 5-year thesis.
stw.name: MU
